0d9649ca4.author: oliverlavery
0d9649ca4.body:
Setting configENABLE_HEAP_PROTECTOR to 1 obfuscates heap
block pointers by XORing them with an application supplied
canary value. This obfuscation helps to catch heap corruption
should a heap buffer overflow occur.

This PR also adds heap bounds checking to heap_4 and heap_5.

This PR also adds some additional integer underflow checks.

7081e76f5.author: Ahmed Ismail
7081e76f5.body:
* copyright-checker: Add FreeRTOS Arm collab copyright

FreeRTOS Arm collab files shall have both Amazon's
and Arm's copyright headers. Hence, the copyright
checker is modified to check for both copyrights.

Signed-off-by: Gaurav Aggarwal <aggarg@amazon.com>

* armv8-m: Add support for IAR with TFM FREERTOS PORT

As the case for ARMClang, and GCC toolchains, IAR
with TFM FreeRTOS Port support is added.

Signed-off-by: Ahmed Ismail <Ahmed.Ismail@arm.com>

* armv8-m: Do not overwrite Control register value

The current ARMv8-M FreeRTOS-Kernel Port code
implementation is modified in a way that allows
the CONTROL register's value to be retained
rather than being overwritten.

This is needed for adding PACBTI support as
the special-purpose CONTROL register `PAC_EN`,
`UPAC_EN`, `BTI_EN`, and `UBTI_EN` PACBTI
enablement bits should be configured before calling
`vRestoreContextOfFirstTask()` function which currently
overwrite the value inside the CONTROL register.

Signed-off-by: Ahmed Ismail <Ahmed.Ismail@arm.com>

* armv8.1-m: Add PACBTI support to kernel non-secure implementation

In this commit, Pointer Authentication, and Branch Target
Identification Extension (PACBTI) support is added for
Non-Secure and Non-TrustZone variants of Cortex-M85
FreeRTOS-Kernel Port.

The PACBTI support is added for Arm Compiler For
Embedded, and IAR toolchains only. The support in
the kernel is not yet enabled for GNU toolchain
due to known issues.

Signed-off-by: Ahmed Ismail <Ahmed.Ismail@arm.com>

* Fix CI check

Signed-off-by: Gaurav Aggarwal <aggarg@amazon.com>

82be77995.author: Gaurav-Aggarwal-AWS
82be77995.body:
* Heap improvements

This commit makes the following improvements:

1. Add a check to heap_2 to track if a memory block is allocated to the
   application or not. The MSB of the size field is used for this
   purpose. The same check already exists in heap_4 and heap_5. This
   check prevents against double free.

2. Add a new flag configHEAP_CLEAR_MEMORY_ON_FREE to heap_2, heap_4 and
   heap_5. The application writer can set it to 1 in their
   FreeRTOSConfig.h to ensure that a block of memory allocated using
   pvPortMalloc is cleared (i.e. set to zero) when it is freed using
   vPortFree. If left undefined, configHEAP_CLEAR_MEMORY_ON_FREE
   defaults to 0 for backward compatibility. We recommend setting
   configHEAP_CLEAR_MEMORY_ON_FREE to 1 for better security.

3. Add a new API pvPortCalloc to heap_2, heap_4 and heap_5. This API
   has the following signature:
   void * pvPortCalloc( size_t xNum, size_t xSize );
   It allocates memory for an array of xNum objects each of which is of
   xSize and initializes all bytes in the allocated storage to zero. If
   allocation succeeds, it returns a pointer to the lowest byte in the
   allocated memory block. On failure, it returns a null pointer.

Signed-off-by: Gaurav Aggarwal <aggarg@amazon.com>
82be77995.subject: Heap improvements (#462)

97050a17a.author: kar-rahul-aws
97050a17a.body:
Memory Protection Unit (MPU) Enhancements

This commit introduces a new MPU wrapper that places additional
restrictions on unprivileged tasks. The following is the list of changes
introduced with the new MPU wrapper:

1. Opaque and indirectly verifiable integers for kernel object handles:
   All the kernel object handles (for example, queue handles) are now
   opaque integers. Previously object handles were raw pointers.

2. Saving the task context in Task Control Block (TCB): When a task is
   swapped out by the scheduler, the task's context is now saved in its
   TCB. Previously the task's context was saved on its stack.

3. Execute system calls on a separate privileged only stack: FreeRTOS
   system calls, which execute with elevated privilege, now use a
   separate privileged only stack. Previously system calls used the
   calling task's stack. The application writer can control the size of
   the system call stack using new configSYSTEM_CALL_STACK_SIZE config
   macro.

4. Memory bounds checks: FreeRTOS system calls which accept a pointer
   and de-reference it, now verify that the calling task has required
   permissions to access the memory location referenced by the pointer.

5. System call restrictions: The following system calls are no longer
   available to unprivileged tasks:
    - vQueueDelete
    - xQueueCreateMutex
    - xQueueCreateMutexStatic
    - xQueueCreateCountingSemaphore
    - xQueueCreateCountingSemaphoreStatic
    - xQueueGenericCreate
    - xQueueGenericCreateStatic
    - xQueueCreateSet
    - xQueueRemoveFromSet
    - xQueueGenericReset
    - xTaskCreate
    - xTaskCreateStatic
    - vTaskDelete
    - vTaskPrioritySet
    - vTaskSuspendAll
    - xTaskResumeAll
    - xTaskGetHandle
    - xTaskCallApplicationTaskHook
    - vTaskList
    - vTaskGetRunTimeStats
    - xTaskCatchUpTicks
    - xEventGroupCreate
    - xEventGroupCreateStatic
    - vEventGroupDelete
    - xStreamBufferGenericCreate
    - xStreamBufferGenericCreateStatic
    - vStr…
97050a17a.subject: Memory Protection Unit (MPU) Enhancements (#705)

0111f1e3102.author: shenmeng
0111f1e3102.body:
Set Privileged Access Never, on taking an exception to EL1.
0b0 PSTATE.PAN is set to 1 on taking an exception to EL1.
0b1 The value of PSTATE.PAN is left unchanged on taking an exception to EL1.
In a system where the PE resets into EL1, this field resets to an
architecturally UNKNOWN value.

If run fuchsia on Qualcomm Snapdragon-710，the bit is not
set for any processor other than processor 0.When processor 1
runs usrboot and trap into the kernel accessing the userspace
address, it gets stuck.If this bit is set for all processors,
then fuchsia works correctly.Run Fuchsia on the development board
vim2. The initial value of this bit is 1, so there is no problem.

0e83c15f5d0.author: Benjamin Prosnitz
0e83c15f5d0.body:
This CL is an implementation of RFC-0080 and adds support for
determining if a VMO child was created in an immutable state (and will
remain immutable).

For this to be the case, the VMO clone must be created with the
ZX_VMO_CHILD_NO_WRITE and ZX_VMO_CHILD_SNAPSHOT flags and without the
ZX_VMO_CHILD_RESIZABLE flag. The child VMO also must not be discardable,
but there currently is no way to create a discardable child (and
therefore also no way to test for this case).

11b0d6dc164.author: Venkatesh Srinivas
11b0d6dc164.body:
Enhanced IBRS is a feature that indicates a processor prefers IBRS to
retpolines to protect code from Branch Target Injection and that IBRS
can be set once early in boot (does not need to be toggled on entry to
protected code).

AMD processors have an equivalent, 'always-on IBRS' and 'prefer IBRS'.
The combination of both bits is very similar to Intel's Enhanced IBRS.

When Enhanced IBRS is present, architecturally it must be used instread
of retpolines to mitigate Spectre V2 [1]. Enable it when enhanced_ibrs
is reported and replace kernel retpolines with direct call sequences.

[1]: https://software.intel.com/security-software-guidance/insights/deep-dive-retpoline-branch-target-injection-mitigation
     "Retpoline is known to be an effective branch target injection
      mitigation on Intel processors belonging to family 6 (enumerated
      by the CPUID instruction) that do not have support for enhanced
      IBRS. On processors that support enhanced IBRS, it should be used
      for mitigation instead of retpoline."

      The following sentence notes retpolines are insufficient on Atom
      Goldmont Plus / Tremont CPUs.

Tested:
1. Booted on Intel NUC7CJYH (Goldmont Plus), with IBRS_ALL
   [01642.782759][1700][2930][klog] INFO: Vendor: Intel
   [01642.782763][1700][2930][klog] INFO: Microarch: Goldmont
   [01642.782764][1700][2930][klog] INFO: F/M/S: 6/7a/1
   [01642.782767][1700][2930][klog] INFO: patch_level: 2c
   [01642.782768][1700][2930][klog] INFO: Brand: Intel(R) Celeron(R) J4005 CPU @ 2.00GHz
   ...
   [01642.782786][1700][2930][klog] INFO: Properties: meltdown pti_enabled ssb ssbd ibpb
                                                      ibpb_ctxt_switch ras_fill enhanced_ibrs
                                                      enhanced_ibrs_enabled retpoline
   ...
   $ k cpu rdmsr 0 0x48
   $ k cpu rdmsr 1 0x48
   [00317.892318][1700][2930][klog] INFO: CPU 0 RDMSR 48h val 1h
   [00351.085242][1700][2930][klog] INFO: CPU 1 RDMSR 48h val 1h

   Ran…
11b0d6dc164.subject: [kernel][x86] Spectre V2: Enhanced IBRS: Enable IBRS

170331480ad.author: John Grossman
170331480ad.body:
Remove the remaining size differences between RefCounted classes with
adoption validation disabled vs. enabled.  Checks for whether the
adoption protocol was properly followed now use a pre-adopted sentinel
value for the ref count instead using a separate tag.  The release
version of this code now needs to pay for an atomic store operation to
move from the constructed sentinel value to the post-adopted value of
1 during adoption, instead of it being completely free as it was
previously.

The advantage is that now code can mix debug vs. release versions of
the class without needing to worry about corrupting state in the
process.  The only real different now is that debug versions of code
will perform debug assert checks while release versions will not.

Unit tests have been updated where appropriate to test both the
release and debug version of these classes.  Also, ASSERTs in the
debug versions of these classes are now being checked using the
ASSERT_DEATH functionality of the unit test framework.

One last note;  The internal ref-counted base class has been
re-arranged slightly to make it a bit easier to extend when required.
The ref_count_ member is now protected instead of private, and
derived classes (fbl::RefCounted and fs:VnodeRefCounted) inherit from
it privately.  This allows the derived classes to manipulate the
ref_count_ directly in order to add functionality, but hides the
member from subsequent classes in the hierarchy.

ZX-838: #done
Test: Ran all zircon level unit tests.
Change-Id: Ie451df8ad4272d30cf72be3c152930b2fa8d89ec
170331480ad.subject: [fbl][ref_counted] Remove size differences in RefCounted.

1a434923c30.author: Venkatesh Srinivas
1a434923c30.body:
Certain x86 processors may speculatively execute instructions after
unconditional JMPs/CALLs/RETs. This speculation may be exploitable for
information-disclosure side channels, by executing code speculatively
with attacker-controller register state to bring protected data into
caches and using other cache disclosure mechanisms to leak that data.

Notably unconditional JMP/RET speculative execution may predict
fall-through, which will never happen in program order; so the
speculative execution is not productive.

AMD-SB-1036 and AMD-SB-1037 recommend inserting INT3 after RET on all
AMD processors and INT3 after JMPs (and LFENCE after CALLs) on < fam 19h
processors [4]. This is an analog of ARM's "Straight Line Speculation" issue,
known as sequential speculation; certain Intel processors may also be
affected, for near indirect JMP, near indirect CALL, and certain
sequential instructions after near RETs [2][4].

See https://www.amd.com/system/files/documents/software-techniques-for-managing-speculation.pdf
Mitigation G-5:
* Place an INT3 (RET, JMP) or LFENCE (CALL) after a branch instruction
  (RET, JMP reg/mem/offset, CALL reg/mem/offset) to help prevent possible
  sequential speculation.

Certain Intel processors also recommend INT3 after RETs [4].

Insert INT3 after RETs and unconditional JMPs in kernel assembly code.
Subsequent changes will pass -mharden-sls to mitigate this issue in
compiled code.

[1]: Equivalent Linux patch:
https://lore.kernel.org/lkml/YXqNAJI3NJz3SQue@hirez.programming.kicks-ass.net/T/
https://www.mail-archive.com/linux-kernel@vger.kernel.org/msg2482430.html
and https://www.mail-archive.com/linux-kernel@vger.kernel.org/msg2482259.html ;
note that this _might_ be helpful on Intel processors as well.

[2]: Intel SDM Volume 2
3-142:
Instructions sequentially following a near indirect CALL instruction
(i.e., those not at the target) may be executed speculatively. If software
needs to prevent this (e.g., in order to prevent a speculative execution…
1a434923c30.subject: [kernel][x86] AMD-SB-1036, 1037/Sequential Spec.: Insert INT3 after JMPs/RETs in assembly

1eb5dad7ab7.author: Corey Tabaka
1eb5dad7ab7.body:
Add support for scheduling deadline tasks. Both elastic (fair) tasks
and inelastic (deadline) tasks are supported in a unified framework.

- Updated queuing algorithm to maintain subtree minimum finish time
  in the WAVLTree and to use a partition search to find the earliest
  eligible deadline in O(log n) time complexity.
- Switched the fair queue to use the same partition search to unify
  fair and deadline queue infrastructure and prepare for moving the
  fair algorithm to a WF2Q variant.
- Added deadline queue and updated scheduler logic to handle both fair
  and deadline queuing disciplines.
- Track expected runtime per task (EMA) and use it to improve CPU
  selection, especially for fair scheduled tasks competing with
  deadline workloads.
- Punted on implementing deadline priority inheritance as it requires
  significant changes in upper layers of the kernel. For now, deadline
  scheduled threads exert maximum fair priority pressure during
  PI interactions with fair scheduled threads.

Bug: 33834
Test: Extensive synthetic workload testing, product build burn in
testing, system tests, core tests, and zircon benchmarks.

Change-Id: I5c30105dbefcd68782a30f0592438b8c0eacb0af
1eb5dad7ab7.subject: [kernel][sched] Introduce deadline support in unified scheduler.

22929240365.author: Venkatesh Srinivas
22929240365.body:
L1TF is a recently (mid 2018) discovered speculation-related CPU
vulnerability that affects certain x86 CPUs; it allows hostile code to
read data that is present in the L1 Data Cache, regardless of whether
it should be able to access that data (per supervisor or VMX context
checks).

Enumerate L1TF as present on all Intel CPUs except:
1) Silvermont(one particular model).
2) CPUs with RDCL_NO set in the ARCH_CAPABILTIES MSR

Other Silvermont derivatives and all Saltwell derivatives could also
be added to the safe list if needed.

Tested:
1) QEMU, w/o KVM:
$ k cpu features
[00007.216] 01168:01185> Vendor: Intel
[00007.216] 01168:01185> Microarch: Haswell
[00007.216] 01168:01185> F/M/S: 6/3c/4
[00007.216] 01168:01185> Brand: Intel Core Processor (Haswell)
[00007.216] 01168:01185> Features: fpu sse sse2 sse3 ssse3 sse4.1 sse4.2 mmx fxsr xsave aesni clflush smep smap erms
[00007.216] 01168:01185> syscall nx rdtscp hypervisor

[00005.303] 01168:01185> Features: fpu sse sse2 sse3 ssse3 sse4.1 sse4.2 mmx fxsr xsave aesni clflush smap syscall

23089061850.author: Joshua Seaton
23089061850.body:
The section may statically be writable, but in any case after relocation
it should be read-only. This change ensures that by updating the layout
to place .dynamic in rodata if it was originally read-only or in RELRO
if writable.

This fixes a regression with BFD on arm64 and x86 where .dynamic was
writable, leading to a writable rodata segment.

26d0276e1aa.author: John Grossman
26d0276e1aa.body:
Add a central point where sets of resource ranges may be registered by
the platform in order to deny requests for user mode access, even when
the calling code has access to the root resource.  Regions must be
added after heap init and global ctors, but before the system becomes
multithreaded.

Currently, platform specific code adds MMIO deny regions for the IO
APIC registers, the Local APIC register, and the IOMMU (on x64), and
the GIC registers (on arm64).  All memory regions registered with the
PMM will be added using common code.

After all of this, the reserved regions of RAM are subtracted out of
the set.  This is currently seems to be needed because RAM used for
communications with the TPM needs to be accessed by user mode code,
and the only signal we currently seem to be getting from the
bootloader are the reserved regions of the memconfig.

Also, actually implement RAM reservations using the newly added
kernel.ram.reserve cmdline argument.  Reserved RAM regions can be used
to create VMOs using zx_vmo_create_physical and the root resource.
These reservations are needed to support core VMO testing, and
typically will never be used in a production system, only in the core
tests zbi_test environment.

This is a blunt tool.  There are other regions which might be reserved
which user mode should not have access to, but our only choice is to
allow them all.  In general, it would be best to (over time) either:

1) Move to a model where these "allow" carve outs are explicitly
   communicated to us using the  ZBI.
2) Or, go one step further and require that _all_ MMIO ranges that
   user mode is permitted to access are explicitly communicated to us
   via the ZBI.

Tests: new "k ut resource" unittest and updated core vmo tests.

2bc67124462.author: Venkatesh Srinivas
2bc67124462.body:
MDS, 'microarchitectural data sampling', is a family of speculative
execution vulnerabilities affecting many Intel x86 CPUs. The
vulnerabilities allow hostile code to infer the contents of recent
loads or stores, without regard to privilege boundaries (user/kernel
and hypervisor guest/host). Hostile code can only read values, it
cannot control addresses that are read.

Certain forms of MDS can be exploited across concurrently-executing
hyperthreads.

The mitigation(s) consist of multiple parts:
1) Flush all microarchitectural structures that can be sampled on
   kernel->user (and hypervisor host -> guest) transitions
2) For certain variants of MDS, either disable hyperthreading or
   flush structures on hyperthread activate/deactivate. (Specifically,
   'store buffers' are statically partitioned and reconfigured
   when threads activate/deactivate).
3) For certain variants, disable hyperthreading or ensure that
   code from multiple mutually-distrusting privilege levels cannot
   execute on hyperthreads of the same physical core concurrently.

This commit only flushes microarchitectural structures on exiting from
kernel mode to user mode.

It does not:
* Deal with hyperthreading, either for kernel/user workloads or for
  kernel/hypervisor workloads.
* Deal with microarchitectural reconfigurations on thread sleep state
  entry/exit.
* Flush structures on VMENTRY

This commit does _not_ turn on the mitigation by default, even when
required; pass kernel.x86.md_clear_on_user_return=true to the kernel
enable the mitigation where required.

Performance change:
https://docs.google.com/spreadsheets/d/11YJ0j0JIk5QNfA8dUOjT0fUSL7PilwKOQ07jlz2YuXs/edit#gid=0
716 test cases in total
510 test cases had no significant difference (no_sig_diff)
0 test cases got faster
206 test cases got slower
0 test cases added
0 test cases removed

ClockGetMonotonic (for example) regresses from 126 ns -> 348 ns, consistent with kernel
exits and interrupts becoming more expensive.

3d0d8e14c2a.author: Venkatesh Srinivas
3d0d8e14c2a.body:
This is a _partial_ mitigation for RETbleed / Branch Type Confusion
on AMD Zen 2 processors. Family 15h and Zen 1 processors are not
mitigated in this commit.

AMD Family 15h, Zen, and Zen 2 processors are affected by RETbleed [1]
and a generalization, Branch Type Confusion [2]. Processors may
mispredict the _type_ of an instruction (No Branch, Direct, Indirect,
RET) and/or the target of the instruction; there is a window between
the misprediction and a redirect to the correct type and target. Type
mispredictions may be caused maliciously and the window between the
misprediction and redirect may be exploited to bring data into caches
and to leak or infer the contents of that data.

Only certain types of type mispredictions (actual type vs predicted
type) may have a window large enough to exploit. See [2] Table-2.

0) Enable STIBP - Single-Thread Indirect Branch Predictor
   This by itself doesn't prevent Branch Type Confusion, but is a
   prerequisite for any BTC-RET mitigations (jmp2ret or IBPB on
   kernel entry).

1) Set MSR DE_CFG2[SuppressBPOnNonBr] to mitigate BTC-NOBR cases

Fuchsia already had mitigations for certain BTC cases; these were added
for BTC and for prior speculative execution issues; see [2] table-3:
- fxrev.dev/693722 and fxrev.dev/661362 added INT3's after RET/direct
  jumps ('SLS protection' in Table 3)
- Fuchsia already uses Retpolines on AMD Zen / Zen 2 processors
  ('IBRS/Retpoline' in Table 3)
- Fuchsia already enables SMEP & stuffs the Return Address Stack
  on context switches ('RAP protection' in Table 3')

Remaining cases / mitigations required:
- BTC-RET (Mispredicting the type or target of RET) - jmp2ret or
  IBPB on kernel entry;
  In addition, defense-in-depth mechanisms may make it difficult
  to exploit BTC-RET - fxrev.dev/710262 tracks zeroing used
  registers before RET, limiting state an attacker can control.
  See [2] section 7.1
- BTC-DIR (Mispredicting the type or target of direct branches)

[1]: https://comsec.ethz.ch/rese…
3d0d8e14c2a.subject: [kernel][x86] AMD Zen 2: Mitigate RETbleed (STIBP, SuppressBPOnNonBr)

42c2dd27a71.author: Venkatesh Srinivas
42c2dd27a71.body:
ARM8 microprocessors may have indirect branch prediction structures,
which can be poisoned to force indirect branches to jump to
destinations that were not intended by the surrounding program and
arguments. Side effects of these misspeculated paths may be observable
by hostile code, ultimately leaking data to contexts that would not
normally be able to access it.

To protect against branch predictor poisoning attacks, microprocessors
may be able to flush the entire attackable structure. System software
may be able to do so by:
1) Executing any SMC call (including PSCI_VERSION) or
2) Executing new PSCI calls (ARCH_WORKAROUND_1) to enumerate and flush
   prediction structures (more efficiently than (1))

Add definitions for the new PSCI calls; this CL does not yet wire them
up nor does it add interfaces to use the new (more efficient) SMCCC
1.1 calling convention.

See: https://github.com/ARM-software/arm-trusted-firmware/pull/1240 and
     "Firmware Interfaces for mitigating CVE_2017_5715"

Bug: 12540 Speculative Execution Mitigations.
Bug: 33667 Spectre mitigations?

46c16e4d1b3.author: Venkatesh Srinivas
46c16e4d1b3.body:
Certain x86 processors (AMD: < fam 19h, some Intel) may speculatively
execute instructions after unconditional JMPs/CALLs/RETs. This
speculation may be exploitable for information-disclosure side channels,
by executing code speculatively with attacker-controller register state
to bring protected data into caches and using other cache disclosure
mechanisms to leak that data.

Inserting serializing instructions (such as INT3) after JMPs/RETs
will mitigate the vulnerability; fxrev.dev/661362 did so for hand-written
assembly, this change does so for compiled code. (Passing -mharden-sls
to the compiler).

3161050   67296  663232 3891578  3b617a out/terminal.x64/kernel_x64/zircon.elf
after:

3165323   67296  663232 3895851  3b722b out/terminal.x64/kernel_x64/zircon.elf

Vmo/Clone/MapParent8192segments/32768kbytes.map                               slower            1.001-1.022    13711255 +/- 83858 ns  13866118 +/- 54421 ns

4e4db1968f2.author: Venkatesh Srinivas
4e4db1968f2.body:
On entry to an interrupt, clear all general purpose registers (after saving
them!); clearing the registers prevents running kernel code with stale,
potentially-hostile values in registers. Clearing stale values from GPRs
in particular confines what values speculative execution in-kernel can
use, making user->kernel speculative-execution attacks more difficult.

Recommended by:
1. "SOFTWARE TECHNIQUES FOR MANAGING SPECULATION ON AMD PROCESSORS"
   https://www.amd.com/system/files/documents/software-techniques-for-managing-speculation.pdf
   Mitigation G-1
2. Speculative Dereferencing of Registers: Reviving Foreshadow
   https://arxiv.org/pdf/2008.02307.pdf
   Section 9 Discussion

5ba131bdc6e.body:
The additional user copy variants allow for the caller to decide what
to do if a page fault occurs. Intended use case is for where the caller
needs to drop locks being held so the page fault can be handled.

At the arch level this is implemented by using the high bit of the
fault return pointer to indicate whether the return should be done
before or after calling the page fault handler.

5fb3ad64cb4.author: Venkatesh Srinivas
5fb3ad64cb4.body:
SSB, 'Speculative Store Bypass', is a speculative execution information
leak bug that poisons memory disambiguation structures to cause loads to
forward data from prior unintended stores. The family of bugs are present
on many AMD and Intel x86 CPUs and at least some ARM CPUs. The
vulnerability _may_ be confined to leaking information within a privilege
level.

Enumerate whether SSB is present on x86 CPUs. As with other bugs
enumerated by Fuchsia, use static tables and override them when
IA32_ARCH_CAPABILITIES or CPUID Fn(8000_0008) EBX[26] are present.

Bug: 36086

Original report:
https://bugs.chromium.org/p/project-zero/issues/detail?id=1528

values, which could trick it into revealing protected data.

67710e8f697.author: Venkatesh Srinivas
67710e8f697.body:
A new speculative execution information leak was just released, 'TAA'.
On various Intel x86 CPUs, it leverages Transaction Aborts to leak
transient/inflight data (recent loads or stores); the vulnerability is
related to MDS, but uses a different disclosure mechanism and appears
to be a much higher data rate leak channel.

Mitigate TAA by applying the MDS mitigation, even if MDS was fixed.
This only changes behaviour on a handful of processors:
* Intel Whiskey Lake Refresh (6/8E/C), Coffee Lake Refresh (6/9E/D)
* Intel Cascade Lake (6/55/6, 7)

https://www.intel.com/content/www/us/en/security-center/advisory/intel-sa-00270.html
CVE-2019-11135

Bug: 33894 Mitigate MDS vulnerabilities
Bug: 12540 Speculative Execution Mitigations.

67710e8f697.subject: [kernel][x86] Mitigate new 'TSX Asynchronous Abort' (TAA) vulnerability

6960b497d70.author: Venkatesh Srinivas
6960b497d70.body:
'STIBP' (Single Thread Indirect Branch Predictor) mode is an x86
mode that causes a processor to segregate indirect branch predictors/
branch target predictors by logical processor. Predictions learned on
one logical processor / hyperthread cannot affect predictions on the
other, regardless of priviledge level.

STIBP is required for a complete Spectre V2 mitigation, for example
when kernel code or sensitive user code is running on one HT and
hostile code on the other one.

Certain processors may have low-overhead/zero overhead STIBP modes.
On these (AMD) processors, enable STIBP once at boot. From testing
on Zen2, there is no perf impact to zircon_benchmarks for doing so,
so we might as well have complete Spectre V2 protection there.

See:
https://developer.amd.com/wp-content/resources/Architecture_Guidelines_Update_Indirect_Branch_Control.pdf

Tested:
* Booted on AMD 15h PD, no change
* Booted on AMD 17h model 71h (Zen2), STIBP enabled:
  $ k cpu rdmsr 0 0x48
  [00019.335] 01697:02834> CPU 0 RDMSR 48h val 2h
* Booted on AMD 17h model 71h, ran zircon_benchmarks and zircon perf
  tests; no change:
  Summary counts:
  780 test cases in total
  780 test cases had no significant difference (no_sig_diff)
  https://docs.google.com/spreadsheets/d/1hmaL_rgwc7py3o5DB_0TQYiWAtS8U300m2VhhZOZSlc/

Bug: 12540 Speculative Execution Mitigations.
Bug: 33667 Spectre mitigations?

Change-Id: If742fa33bc4d77e3e59fcffd81116e14ff3245b5
6960b497d70.subject: [kernel][x86] Spectre V2: AMD: Enable STIBP if always-on STIBP is set

6fed4cb5d6d.author: Nick Maniscalco
6fed4cb5d6d.body:
This change adds the lockup_detector library and instruments the
kernel to detect when the kernel has held a spinlock for too long.

When compiled-in and enabled, the lockup detector will emit a KERNEL
OOPS if a CPU holds a spinlock for more than the configured threshold.
The CPU must eventually release the spinlock for detection to occur.
A future change may eliminate this requirement (see
lib/lockup_detector/README.md).

By default, no threshold is set so no lockups will be detected or
reported.  A threshold can be set using the kernel command line
argument `kernel.lockup-detector.threshold-ms`.

The instrumentation is compiled in only when DEBUG_ASSERT_IMPLEMENTED.

Add end-to-end test that triggers a temporary lockup and verifies a
KERNEL OOPS is emitted.

Manual perfcompare runs on Sherlock showed no measurable performance
impact.  Perfcompare bot on dawson canyon perfcompare bot shows a
small impact to some sensitive microbenchmarks even when no threshold
is set.

7d731b4e959.author: Travis Geiselbrecht
7d731b4e959.body:
Previously, was always setting UXN and PXN bits on pages explicitly
mapped as non executable, not taking into account that user (EL0) code
could access a privileged page because UXN wasn't set.

Change the logic to appropriately set PXN and UXN bits on user and
privleged executable pages, appropriately:

user/privileged non-executable page: UXN=1, PXN=1
user executable page: UXN=0, PXN=1
privileged executable page: UXN=1, PXN=0

EL2 mappings for the kernel interpret these bits slightly differently,
so simply map the non executable code as XN=1 (bit 54).

Add kernel unit test to validate that pages mapped this way at least
appear to be in sync with the aspace.Query() api.

8b59957f239.body:
Kernel policy refers to the things that are not
covered by object-capabilities (aka handles). For example
how to restrict channel creation or how to control
memory usage.

We had an exploration of how that would look in the
"bad handle" policy, now its time to get a real version
this time:

- Centered in the job object (only way to set policy)
- Inmutable: the policy applies when processes are created
  and it will never change.

Actually in the CL:

1- remove the "bad handle" policy as it was implemented
2- introduce the new policy visible parts which form
   a set of key-value pairs [topic:policy]
3- introduce two new rights set-policy and get-policy
4- a fake test to drive the point home

8d8b6b1cf9e.author: Venkatesh Srinivas
8d8b6b1cf9e.body:
Spectre V2 attacks by hostile userspace programs against the kernel
rely on mistraining indirect branch predictors to cause the kernel to
speculatively execute any target code and leak sensitive data via
cache side effects.

To mitigate Spectre V2 attacks, we clear indirect branch predictors
at sensitive points - a prior CL did so on context switches to a new
address space. This CR clears predictors in situations where user code
is doing something suspicious (running in userspace with kernel $PC).

Invalidate indirect branch predictors (by executing a PSCI call) on:
* Instruction aborts taken in EL0 with a kernel $PC
* Debug (BRK) aborts taken in EL0 with a kernel $PC
* IRQs taken in EL0 with a kernel $PC

These are circumstances where EL0 code has a sensitive source $PC.

These may not be all sites; these are only the same sites that Linux
addresses.

This mitigation may not be sufficient for ARM cores not from ARM,
Holdings, Ltd. Other ARM cores may require invalidating the (indirect)
branch predictor on every kernel entry or require different
invalidation sequences.

Bug: 33667 Spectre mitigations?

8f6ff41970e.author: Roland McGrath
8f6ff41970e.body:
Even when not compiled with shadow-call-stack (e.g. GCC), this
changes the protocol between the assembly and C code for the
exception paths.  Rather than having C code conditionally reload the
fixed percpu_ptr register when its assembly caller says it came from
a lower EL, all the assembly exception entry points from a lower EL
reload the percpu_ptr register directly themselves.  This seems less
error-prone in general and also removes some branches.  The real
motivation is that the shadow-call-stack unavoidably must always be
reloaded before calling any normal C function (or else every such
callee would need to be annotated to disable shadow-call-stack).
Since the assembly needs to do that anyway, it can reload both fixed
registers with a single ldp instruction.

8f6ff41970e.subject: [kernel] Use shadow-call-stack on ARM64

911dc99ce8e.author: Roland McGrath
911dc99ce8e.body:
This moves the setup of the boot CPU's x86_percpu in %gs.base
into assembly and the most basic initialization of its x86_percpu
into a compile-time static initializer.  This makes it possible
for all the C code on the startup path to use safe-stack and/or
stack-protector (which gets harder and harder to avoid with LTO).

Now the only C function that runs at all is x86_secondary_entry.
The work it does to discover its CPU number (and thus the right
address for its own x86_percpu) is too complex to do in assembly.
Instead, x86_secondary_entry is split so that the first C
function run on secondary CPUs is a simple one that manages to
avoid stack-protector/safe-stack code, and it sets up %gs.base
and enough of x86_percpu that it can then call the second C
function and that function can safely use safe-stack and/or
stack-protector code.

Change-Id: If8f0860a05a0bd98285a15d0f2f97699bf16d9d7
911dc99ce8e.subject: [kernel][arch][x86] Revamp stack-protector/safe-stack early setup

99eca20c97c.author: Venkatesh Srinivas
99eca20c97c.body:
In Fuchsia, all physical memory is mapped through an aperture, the
physmap; this allows the kernel to access any physical page without
going through map/unmap cycles. The physmap is mapped in early boot;
on x86 processors, this constraint (plus NX requiring later enabling
in EFER) meant that the physmap was not marked NX on x86. The ARM64
physmap was already eXecute-never.

During late boot, mark all arena-backed regions of the physmap as
no-execute; this would prevent:
* Inadvertent (or malicious) jumps into code in the physmap
* Speculative execution of code in the physmap

9e7907a8efb.author: Venkatesh Srinivas
9e7907a8efb.body:
* L1TF is a speculative execution information leak vulnerability that
  allows a VM guest to infer the contents of any physical address in
  the system that is present in the L1 Data Cache.

* MDS is a speculative execution information leak vulnerability that
  allows a VM guest or userspace to infer the contents of recent loads
  and stores. It has a variant, TAA, which uses TSX to more efficiently
  infer the same contents.

Mitigate L1TF and MDS in the case of VM guests by flushing the L1 Data
Cache on VM entry. If L1TF is not present but MDS is (Silvermont, Coffee
Lake 9th gen), issue an MDS buffer overwrite sequence instead.

Bug: 33894 Mitigate MDS vulnerabilities
Bug: 12540 Speculative Execution Mitigations.
Bug: 33667 Spectre mitigations?

Change-Id: I59cc5c5c4249c9d5e71ff020c2c89a00260ac0ba
9e7907a8efb.subject: [kernel][x86] vmm: Flush L1D$ or MDS buffers on VM Entry (L1TF, MDS)

a08c216af77.body:
In order to support the full range of services that are available via
the ARM Secure Monitor, zx_smc_call needed to gain some additional input
and output parameters. These additional parameters bring the syscall in
line with the ARM SMC Calling Convention where there are eight input
registers and four output registers.

The SMC invocation itself was moved out from underneath the PSCI kernel
driver to the arm64 arch specific component. All current SMC invocations
were updated to the new function and there are currently no calls from
higher layers.

a08c216af77.subject: [syscalls] Add ARM SMCCC support to SMC syscall

a20d84bcaa3.author: Venkatesh Srinivas
a20d84bcaa3.body:
Spectre V1 is a family of speculative execution information leak bugs
that rely on mistraining a conditional branch predictor into skipping
checks and then using the side effects of wrong-path speculative
executions to infer the values of data that cannot be read normally
by an attacker. The family of attacks affects a large number of CPU
families from multiple vendors, both x86 and aarch64 varieties.

Mitigating Spectre V1 involves converting sensitive checks from
branch-based sequences to data-dependent branchless variants or
introducing speculation barriers where appropriate.

Introduce conditional_select_nospec_*() to select between one of two
integral arguments based on whether a predicate is true or false,
even in wrong-path speculative executions. This will simplify
hardening code that selects between a Handle that was looked-up that
matches the current process or a safe/null handle, for example.

The predicates provided are _eq (testing whether two arguments are
equal) and _lt (testing whether a first argument is less than the
second, using an unsigned comparison).

Tested:
runtests -t fbl-test -- --gtest_filter Conditional*

Bug: 12540 Speculative Execution Mitigations.
Bug: 33667 Spectre mitigations?

Change-Id: Ie7a67506b53a9a64b8ac65f1313f174357ba01c5
a20d84bcaa3.subject: [fbl]: conditional_select_nospec_*: Safely select between values vs Spectre V1

a44b73c3b03.author: Venkatesh Srinivas
a44b73c3b03.body:
Handle::FromU32() was vulnerable to a Spectre-V1 bounds check bypass
attack, on the handle index bounds check. Harden it to protect
downstream code.

Old code (slightly simplified):
1  Handle* Handle::FromU32(uint32_t value) {
2    uintptr_t handle_addr = IndexToHandle(value & kHandleIndexMask);
3    if (unlikely(!HandleTableArena::arena_.Committed(handle_addr) {
4      return nullptr;
5    }
6    auto handle = reinterpret_cast<Handle*>(handle_addr);
7    return likely(handle->base_value() == value) ? handle : nullptr;
8  }

If the branch outcome at line (3) were mispredicted, we would continue
executing code at line 6 and 7. Handle::base_value() would execute with
an attacker-controlled value of |handle|. The caller,
ProcessDispatcher::GetHandleLocked() passed the returned handle to a
number of sites, some of which use values from within the structure
for further de-references. For example, sys_object_wait_many() uses
the Handle's dispatcher_ field as a valid dispatcher and invokes
Dispatcher::AddObserver in WaitStateObserver::Begin(), serving as a
Spectre V1 leak gadget.

Harden the committed check of the handle - rather than relying on
the branch against the return of Committed() alone, use
fbl::confine_array_index to use entry 0 in wrong-path speculation
instead.

This CR does not handle the second branch in Handle::From32(), the
check of Handle::base_value() against the passed-in handle index.

Bug: 12540 Speculative Execution Mitigations.
Bug: 33667 Spectre mitigations?

Change-Id: Ic12456ec906d83ac4f424fe7b5fe23947a82b6e8
a44b73c3b03.subject: [kernel] Spectre V1: Harden handle arena bounds check

a4e36b890c4.author: Venkatesh Srinivas
a4e36b890c4.body:
SSB, 'Speculative Store Bypass', is a speculative execution information
leak bug that poisons memory disambiguation structures to cause loads to
forward data from prior unintended stores. The family of bugs are present
on many AMD and Intel x86 CPUs and at least some ARM CPUs. The vulnerability
is confined to leaking data within a privilege level.

Add mitigations for AMD and Intel x86 CPUs, by using vendor- and CPU-specific
memory disambiguation disable controls.

The mitigation has a negative performance impact. For example,
Channel/WriteRead/1024 regresses by 2 - 13% on Skylake, 7 - 11% on Coffee Lake.

This mitigation is disabled by default. To enable (where needed), set
kernel.x86.spec_store_bypass_disable=true.

Tested:
1.0 Boot tests:
1.1 Booted on AMD 17h ZP-B1 (Ryzen), ran kernel unit tests
1.2 Booted on Intel NUC6i5SYH (Skylake), ran kernel unit tests

[1447.751] 01419.01421> CPU 1 RDMSR 48h val 4h
[1449.856] 01419.01421> CPU 2 RDMSR 48h val 4h
[1451.449] 01419.01421> CPU 3 RDMSR 48h val 4h
1.3 Booted on Intel NUC7CJYH (Goldmont+), ran kernel unit tests [1000 iterations]
Checked IA32_SPEC_CTRL:
[4168.003] 01421.01423> CPU 0 RDMSR 48h val 4h	(4 == bit(2), SSBD)
[4178.852] 01421.01423> CPU 1 RDMSR 48h val 4h
1.4 Booted on AMD 15h OR-C0, ran kernel unit tests

https://docs.google.com/spreadsheets/d/1t6v6_s8-LE-XoZVvE4VZsuyTv9ywmlCJcfixxbSHtoE

716 test cases in total
486 t…

a6f2e6fe2f7.author: Venkatesh Srinivas
a6f2e6fe2f7.body:
The low-level syscall code on ARM64 roughly does:
  arm64_syscall_dispatcher
  ... (x16 == syscall #, user input)
  1: if (x16 >= #ZX_SYS_COUNT)
  2:   jmp .Lunknown_syscall
  3: x12 <= &.Lsyscall_table
  4: x12 <= x12 + x16 << 4
  5: jmp x12

If x16 is >= ZX_SYS_COUNT but a conditional branch predictor mispredicts
the outcome of the branch at (1), the kernel will speculatively index
into the syscall table based on a user-provided index. This could be
leveraged into an information disclosure gadget for data after
.Lsyscall_table in memory.

Add a conditional select + CSDB to convert out-of-range syscall numbers
to 0; this is after the test / branch at (2), so it will never change
the value of the syscall number in program execution, only in
wrong-path speculative execution.

This was done for x86 in commit eedaf885d6b via an LFENCE.

b4135eda402.author: Venkatesh Srinivas
b4135eda402.body:
x86 processors consult a hardware stack to predict target addresses of
RET instructions. The behavior of RAS/RSBs are well-documented, so they
are used as a core structure in Spectre V2 indirect branch target poisoning
defenses (retpolines); however RSBs themselves may be attacked and are
sometimes susceptible to underflow (SpectreRSB and Skylake underflow).

To protect against RSB attacks (cross-process and user->kernel), we
conservatively overwrote the RSB on all kernel entries in an earlier
commit.

That was unnecessary - potentially poisoned RSB entries can only be
consumed by RETs not paired with CALLs. This situation is only possible
in the context switch code and on #VMExits. Move the RSB flush out of
the kernel entries to the context switch code.

Also add a TODO to the x86_mwait() idle code - certain idle states clear
the RSB and open underflow attacks.

Bug: 33667 Spectre mitigations?
Bug: 12540 Speculative Execution Mitigations.

Change-Id: I67d1d0e18eb64581f8faa77920f486b457ee4da1
b4135eda402.subject: [kernel][x86] Spectre V2: Flush RAS on context switches, not every kernel entry

bcc0f88de12.author: Corey Tabaka
bcc0f88de12.body:
Initial implementation of critical deadline run queues produced
primarily by Jetski.

This change has been reviewed and tweaked by the author for correctness
and consistency.

The following changes are made:

- Validation and ingestion of the new critical deadline flag when
  creating a profile, with test confirming validation logic.
- Updating base and effective profiles to include a critical flag.
- Updating the bandwidth PI logic to track upstream critical flag counts
  to correctly set the critical flag in the effective profile of the
  target thread.
- Adding a critical deadline run queue and updating queue/dequeue logic
  and next thread evaluation to account for the critical flag in under
  and oversubscribed conditions.
- Adding a test to verify the precedence of critical deadline threads in
  the oversubscribed condition.

d49ef9856bc.author: Venkatesh Srinivas
d49ef9856bc.body:
Enable generation of calls to sized-delete operators; when a compiler
knows that an object being deallocated has a particular size or alignment,
it can call a version of the delete operator with the size specified.

Some allocators can optimize for this path and avoid header lookups;
our kernel heap allocator cannot - its structure would make this
somewhat difficult. Instead, assert that the passed-in size is <= the
size in the malloc block's header. This would guard against certain
corruption of the allocator free list - if we free an object bigger
than its header, we may have underran our buffer.

This assert has very minimal perf costs on x86 - most perfcompare tests
are unaffected, the ones that are are affected marginally.

d7689a848a0.author: Venkatesh Srinivas
d7689a848a0.body:
MDS (and TAA) are a family of speculative execution vulnerabilities
that allow the contents of recent loads and stores to be inferred across
privilege boundaries. Attackers can infer the contents but cannot
control the addresses that are accessed. Attacks can be carried out
across concurrently-executing hyperthreads.

To mitigate MDS in the non-hyperthread case, the kernel must flush
microarchitectural buffers on privilege transitions (Kernel -> User,
Kernel -> VM Guest). We have had the code to do so for some time;
per our security/zircon meeting, set the default to enable MDS where
required. This default matches Linux/ChromeOS.

Perf difference (vs baseline):
* Perfcompare shows no difference, but that is because our bots
  have an old BIOS/microcode that pre-dates MDS/MD_CLEAR

  Local testing w/ NUC7i3BNH is consistent w/ MD_CLEAR adding
  200 - 250 nS to the kernel -> user exit on KBL-U.

  https://docs.google.com/spreadsheets/d/1ZgeyZEUmMELiOC7G4mhpTfivlS11fBhiuREfLCcRm4A

d7da0611a97.author: Venkatesh Srinivas
d7da0611a97.body:
Spectre V1 information leak attacks (against the kernel) generally
cause trusted code to speculatively run with untrusted inputs, to
read sensitive data into L1 caches, and then use different mechanisms
to leak data that is present in L1 caches to hostile code. Often
hostile code passes invalid or out-of-range indexes to trusted code
and relies on skipping bounds check branches, to feed those indexes
forward into more trusted code.

Most processors affected by Spectre V1 only allow leaking data that
is present in L1 / inner caches.

This commit flushes the L1 data cache on every kernel exit for a
syscall entry, when the syscall would return ZX_ERR_INVALID_ARGS or
ZX_ERR_BAD_HANDLE.

We only flush on certain errors - errors related to invalid args that
are often part of Spectre V1 attack sequences. We only flush on errors
that are not expected in the steady state, so this mitigation should
have minimal performance impact.

This mitigation raises the bar (substantially) to Spectre V1 attacks
but does not outright prevent them. Kernel preemption, attacks w/
valid syscall arguments, or attacks that cause non-handled errors
are still possible.

Bug: 33667 Spectre mitigations?
Bug: 12540 Speculative Execution Mitigations.

Change-Id: Ie366e6a1f2fe7a05e048cbe8994be652967f9e08
d7da0611a97.subject: [kernel][x86] Spectre V1: Flush L1D$/all caches on some syscall errors

Before invoking (C++) syscall handlers in kernel, clear any
user registers not containing arguments. This prevents
in-kernel speculative execution from using any user-controlled
values, which could trick it into revealing protected data.

Based on fxrev.dev/689047, which added this mitigation to x86.

e0839c6c241.author: Venkatesh Srinivas
e0839c6c241.body:
arch_copy_from_user is invoked to copy from user buffers into kernel
memory. It is invoked with user-supplied |src,len|, but the function
checks that both src and src+len are valid user addresses.

Under speculation, it is possible for hostile user code to bypass
the address range check and pass in kernel |src,len| addresses. If this
happens, it is possible that wrong-path speculation will copy from
a kernel address range to the destination buffer. If there are
dependent accesses on the contents of the buffer, they may leak the
contents of kernel memory.

For example, consider sys_port_queue() - if the port_packet were
copied from kernel memory and any code in PortDispatcher::QueueUser /
::Queue examined the contents of the copied-in packet and used that to
look into any tables, the cache side effects of the second dependent
access may be visible and leak information about the original kernel
buffer.

Harden copy_from_user to confine the src buffer to user memory;
ensure the entire buffer is below the top of the user address range.

On the x86-64 we did this in commit 83d7b2215a7e by adding an LFENCE.

e621cb36f3e.author: John Grossman
e621cb36f3e.body:
Define a type, and a parameter structure for ARM SMMUs which can be
used with the `zx_iommu_create` syscall.

Currently, systems with SMMUs are simply creating a single DummyIommu
instance, and then creating BTIs with fake StreamIDs.  This is fine
for operating with the system's SMMUs disabled, or when it is
preconfigured to put all of the required initiators into bypass mode,
but more needs to be done in the long run.

Eventually, user-mode is going to need to obtain handles to all of the
actual SMMU instances in the system, and then create BTIs using the
actual stream IDs (as reported in the device tree) under the correct
SMMU instances.

This small patch should allow for a graceful transition to that mode.
Users can now explicitly request a handle to an SMMU instance with the
desired instance uniquely identified by the physical base address of
the specific SMMU instance.  Under the hood, we are simply going to
return an instance of a DummyIommu, meaning that the system should
continue to operate exactly as it does today, allowing the plumbing to
create the proper SMMU and BTI instances to be developed in parallel
with developing support for the SMMU hardware itself.

e6e2db9ecd5.author: Dave Bort
e6e2db9ecd5.body:
Disabled by default; use kernel commandline "kernel.oom.enable=true" or
shell command "k oom start" to enable. Use "k oom lowmem" to trigger a
fake low-memory condition. (See docs/kernel_cmdline.md and "k oom" for
details)

Every second, checks the amount of free PMM memory, and kills a job
(that has running processes) if the free amount is less than
kernel.oom.redline-mb (10MB by default).

When killing, prints info about the larger memory-using processes and
the list of jobs it considers for killing. E.g.:

  OOM: oom_lowmem(shortfall_bytes=524288) called
  OOM: Process mapped committed bytes:
  OOM:   proc  1043    9M 'bin/devmgr'
  OOM:   proc  1861   24M 'devhost:pci#3:8086:2922'
  OOM: Finding a job to kill...
  OOM:   (skip) job   1092 'fuchsia'
  OOM:   *KILL* job   1091 'magenta-services'
  OOM:        + proc  1093  run 'crashlogger'
  OOM:        + proc  1192  run 'virtual-console'
  OOM:        + proc  1219  run 'netsvc'
  OOM:        + proc  2062  run 'sh:console'
  OOM:        + proc  2140  run 'vc:sh'
  OOM:        + proc  2210  run 'vc:sh'
  OOM:        + proc  2245  run 'vc:sh'
  OOM:        = 7 running procs (7 total), 0 jobs
  OOM:   (next) job   1077 'magenta-drivers'
  OOM:   (next) job   1028 'root'
  OOM:   (next) job   1024 '<superroot>'

e6f9d966c79.author: Venkatesh Srinivas
e6f9d966c79.body:
Add boot-time option to zero kernel heap allocations at allocation
time, to mitigate cases where we inadvertently read from or leak
uninitialized memory.

The feature defaults-off until its performance/security tradeoff
can be explored more completely.

To enable, set kernel.heap.alloc_fill_threshold.

Tested:
(w/ 256 byte threshold):

1) perfcompare (Dawson Canyon NUC):
   A number of tests slowed down, in the 10 - 60 nSec range
   Summary counts:
     2942 test cases in total
     2890 test cases had no significant difference (no_sig_diff)
     3 test cases got faster
     49 test cases got slower
     0 test cases added
     0 test cases removed
   https://logs.chromium.org/logs/fuchsia/buildbucket/cr-buildbucket.appspot.com/8853802975950605360/+/u/compare_perf_test_results_without_and_with_CL/stdout

2) local perfcompare (Intel Goldmont (Atom) NUC6CAYH, 7 boots x 500 iters):
   Summary counts:
     794 test cases in total
     791 test cases had no significant difference (no_sig_diff)
     0 test cases got faster
     3 test cases got slower
     0 test cases added
     0 test cases removed

    Test case                                    Factor change  Mean before     Mean after
    GetInfo/Runtime/P=1/T=1.enumerate_processes  1.026-1.127    1829 +/- 12 ns  1968 +/- 79 ns
    HandleCreate_Event.create                    1.038-1.118    839 +/- 13 ns   904 +/- 20 ns
    ObjectWaitAsync.CreatePort                   1.008-1.153    861 +/- 28 ns   929 +/- 32 ns

    It appears that the impact is small on an Intel Atom ('Goldmont');
    as a low-end/client part, this was interesting. The relative cost
    of doing this mitigation might be lower on low-end SOCs than
    high-end ones.

ed4794c6c4d.author: Todd Eisenberger
ed4794c6c4d.body:
VmAddressRegions are the new VM partitioning layer.  They are
a tree-structured partitioning of the address space in which
each region can be subdivided into additional regions and VM
mappings.  This gives the caller more control over the address
space layout, such as being able to guarantee that parts of the
address space won't be used by anything else.

This patch implements the core of VMARs, but does not expose them
yet.  It includes a compatibility layer in VmAspace for users of
the old interface.

This drops support for min_alloc_gap, which will be unnecessary
once the migration is finished.

This changes the behavior of VMM_FLAG_VALLOC_BASE, to actually
just be a hint to start the search for space near the top of the
user aspace.

The following should be coming in followup patches:
- Restricting invocation of the compatibility layer to the kernel
  address space only
- Exposing new syscalls to allow usermode to manipulate VMARs
- Integrating those syscalls into our process creation flow
- Replacing the allocation scheme within the VMAR code to be randomized
  rather than first-fit placement.

Change-Id: I550a3120320d4f6c4cf72caeb4ed3226c79104f7
ed4794c6c4d.subject: [vm] Replace VmRegions with VmAddressRegions

edd438b8f6f.author: Mark Seaborn
edd438b8f6f.body:
Add a macro, APPLY_CODE_PATCH_FUNC(), which allocates some placeholder
instructions and declares how to replace them with working code on
kernel startup.

The initial use case is filling out the STAC and CLAC instructions
conditionally depending on whether the CPU supports SMAP.

The mechanism is a little more general than this initial use case
requires.  My intention is the mechanism will be usable for inline
assembly that can get inlined into multiple places.

eedaf885d6b.author: Venkatesh Srinivas
eedaf885d6b.body:
The low-level syscall entry point does the following (equivalent)
code to convert a syscall number into a function call through the
system call table:

  1: if (%rax >= $ZX_SYS_COUNT)
  2:   jmp unknown_syscall
  3: %r11 = &call_wrapper_table
  4: *(%r11, %rax, 8)()

  The bounds-check is vulnerable to Spectre V1 (bounds check bypass)
  and the indirect jump is vulnerable to Spectre V2 (branch target
  injection).

Convert the code to a speculation-safe sequence:
  1: if (%rax >= $ZX_SYS_COUNT)
  2:  jmp unknown_syscall
  3: %r11 <= &call_wrapper_table
  4: %r11 <= (%r11, %rax, %r8)
  5: lfence
  6: *(%r11)()

This sequence should be safe against both Spectre V1 and V2 - the branch
target address is computed and separated from the indirect jump by a
dispatch-serializing LFENCE. The LFENCE will stall dispatch until the
direction of the branch at 1: is known and until the address of the
target in %r11 is known.

See: AMD Mitigation V1-1, V2-2

Bug: 12540 Speculative Execution Mitigations.
Bug: 33667 Spectre mitigations?

Change-Id: Icc7e776dcbb84e49acf7e07fd85db221aa7b8551
eedaf885d6b.subject: [kernel][x86] Spectre V1, V2: Harden syscall entry point

f7531f802a6.author: Travis Geiselbrecht
f7531f802a6.body:
Mostly a lot of shuffling around of code to better set up the page fault
and faulting path to be more flexible. Now VMOs will not read-allocate
and instead return a pointer to a single physical page in the system that is
intentionally full of zeros only.

It's not a massive optimization, but it actually forces a refactor of the
fault path to be more in lined with what happens later with true copy-on-write.

f8dec1517a4.author: Marco Vanotti
f8dec1517a4.body:
This CL adds a new variant to our build, allowing us to build the zircon
kernel with undefined behavior sanitizer (UBSan).

You use it by setting `--variant kubsan`.

A thin runtime is added in
`zircon/kernel/lib/instrumentation/ubsan/runtime.cc`.

Note that not all ubsan checks are enabled at this time. Once we grow
the runtime, more checks can be selectively enabled in
`zircon/kernel/lib/instrumentation/ubsan/BUILD.gn`.

This CL also fixes an instance of undefined behavior in `vm/page.h` and
adds debug commands to manually test that the sanitizer instrumentation
is working.

fb357c1b6d1.author: Roland McGrath
fb357c1b6d1.body:
This enables -fsanitize-cfi-cross-dso in the *-cfi variants for
phys.  A custom runtime is supplied for phys modules to indirect
through gSymbolize to a slow-path function.  In physload, this is
enabled with a slow-path function that consults the module list
and dispatches to the appropriate __cfi_check function.

The __cfi_check functions in loaded ELF modules (physboot, kernel
proper) are exported to their .dynsym at link time and then
looked up in ElfImage::Relocate.

ff318e72901.author: Will Drewry
ff318e72901.body:
This changes enables restricted mode on arm64 to run 32-bit restricted
tasks. The vbar is extended to handle exceptions from aarch32 el0 and
enforces that those exceptions only occur in restricted mode threads.

Access to aarch32 is provided by expanding the CPSR to enable the
aarch32 mode, thumb, GE, Q, and IT bits when set by the restricted
state and enables debuggers to see these values (but not set them).

Testing is included via utest/core/restricted-mode through the
addition of a derived register state. A check for support
is also added for non-DUT architectures to ensure aarch32 is
supported before testing.

1d5c9265.author: Damien Zammit
1d5c9265.body:
This adds a working rump driver for /dev/wmX cards,
which are Intel i8254x Gigabit Ethernet devices.
(See man.netbsd.org for "wm(4)")
This should be easily extended to support other NICs
by contributing some makefile foo to netbsd/rump.

TESTED:
  - On UP+apic it works 100% with hurd-i386.
  - On SMP it also works 100% with hurd-i386.

Example usage:
  settrans -fgap /dev/rumpnet /hurd/rumpnet
  settrans -fgap /dev/wm0 /hurd/devnode -M /dev/rumpnet wm0
  settrans -fgap /servers/socket/2 /hurd/pfinet -i /dev/wm0
  ifup /dev/wm0

TODO: check tcpdump support

Requires latest master of librump for nofifofs dynamic lib.

34a94ce8.author: Justus Winter
34a94ce8.body:
Previously, a Subhurd's tasks were shown as weird processes in the
Motherhurd.  This change connects the proc server in the Motherhurd
with the proc server in the Subhurd, embedding the Subhurd's process
hierarchy.  Subhurd's processes can now be inspected and debugged like
any other process.

* NEWS: Update.
* boot/boot.c (mach_msg_forward): New function.
(boot_demuxer): Forward messages arriving on the new task notification
port from the proc server, and forward them to the proc server inside
the Subhurd via the notification port.
* proc/info.c (S_proc_task2proc): Relay request for processes in a task
namespace to the Subhurd's proc server.
(S_proc_pid2proc): Likewise.
(S_proc_getprocargs): Likewise.
(S_proc_getprocenv): Likewise.
(S_proc_getprocinfo): Likewise.  Translate PIDs.
(S_proc_getloginid): Likewise.
(S_proc_getloginpids): Likewise.
* proc/mgt.c (namespace_is_subprocess): New function.
(namespace_translate_pids): Likewise.
* proc/msg.c (S_proc_getmsgport): Relay request for processes in a task
namespace to the Subhurd's proc server.
* proc/pgrp.c (S_proc_getsid): Likewise.  Translate PIDs.
(S_proc_getsessionpids): Likewise.
(S_proc_getsessionpgids): Likewise.
(S_proc_getpgrppids): Likewise.
* proc/proc.h (namespace_is_subprocess): New prototype.
(namespace_translate_pids): Likewise.
34a94ce8.subject: proc: Hierarchical proc servers.

Stack allocation on Xen can fail because of fragmentation. This change

8c0e65b6.author: Justus Winter
8c0e65b6.body:
Previously, boot handed privileged kernel ports to Subhurds.  This
made boot unusable for unprivileged users, and allowed Subhurds to
wreak havoc on the whole system.  Fix this by handing out pseudo ports
instead of the privileged ports.

* NEWS: Update.
* boot/Makefile (MIGSTUBS): Build stubs for the new kernel interfaces.
(MIGSFLAGS): Include mutation file.
(HURDLIBS): Link against libihash.
* boot/boot.c (privileged, want_privileged): New variables.
(pseudo_privileged_host_port): New variable.
(pseudo_pset, pseudo_kernel): Likewise.
(task_notification_port): Likewise.
(dead_task_notification_port): Likewise.
(boot_demuxer): Handle new protocols.
(OPT_PRIVILEGED): New macro.
(options): Add flag '--privileged' to enable the old mode.
(parse_opt): Handle new flag.
(allocate_pseudo_ports): New function.
(main): Handle new flag.  If not running privileged, allocate more
pseudo ports to hand out in place of privileged kernel ports, create a
task namespace, and a task that the Subhurd can frob instead of the
real kernel task.
(do_mach_notify_dead_name): Handle dying tasks.
(S_vm_set_default_memory_manager): New function.
(S_host_reboot): Likewise.
(S_host_processor_set_priv): Likewise.

000d388ed3bb.author: Matthew Garrett
000d388ed3bb.body:
While existing LSMs can be extended to handle lockdown policy,
distributions generally want to be able to apply a straightforward
static policy. This patch adds a simple LSM that can be configured to
reject either integrity or all lockdown queries, and can be configured
at runtime (through securityfs), boot time (via a kernel parameter) or
build time (via a kconfig option). Based on initial code by David
Howells.

0311507792b5.author: Deven Bowers
0311507792b5.body:
Integrity Policy Enforcement (IPE) is an LSM that provides an
complimentary approach to Mandatory Access Control than existing LSMs
today.

Existing LSMs have centered around the concept of access to a resource
should be controlled by the current user's credentials. IPE's approach,
is that access to a resource should be controlled by the system's trust
of a current resource.

The basis of this approach is defining a global policy to specify which
resource can be trusted.

Signed-off-by: Deven Bowers <deven.desai@linux.microsoft.com>
Signed-off-by: Fan Wu <wufan@linux.microsoft.com>
[PM: subject line tweak]
Signed-off-by: Paul Moore <paul@paul-moore.com>

0499680a4214.author: Vasiliy Kulikov
0499680a4214.body:
Add support for mount options to restrict access to /proc/PID/
directories.  The default backward-compatible "relaxed" behaviour is left
untouched.

The first mount option is called "hidepid" and its value defines how much
info about processes we want to be available for non-owners:

hidepid=0 (default) means the old behavior - anybody may read all
world-readable /proc/PID/* files.

hidepid=1 means users may not access any /proc/<pid>/ directories, but
their own.  Sensitive files like cmdline, sched*, status are now protected
against other users.  As permission checking done in proc_pid_permission()
and files' permissions are left untouched, programs expecting specific
files' modes are not confused.

hidepid=2 means hidepid=1 plus all /proc/PID/ will be invisible to other
users.  It doesn't mean that it hides whether a process exists (it can be
learned by other means, e.g.  by kill -0 $PID), but it hides process' euid
and egid.  It compicates intruder's task of gathering info about running
processes, whether some daemon runs with elevated privileges, whether
another user runs some sensitive program, whether other users run any
program at all, etc.

gid=XXX defines a group that will be able to gather all processes' info
(as in hidepid=0 mode).  This group should be used instead of putting
nonroot user in sudoers file or something.  However, untrusted users (like
daemons, etc.) which are not supposed to monitor the tasks in the whole
system should not be added to the group.

hidepid=1 or higher is designed to restrict access to procfs files, which
might reveal some sensitive private information like precise keystrokes
timings:

http://www.openwall.com/lists/oss-security/2011/11/05/3

hidepid=1/2 doesn't break monitoring userspace tools.  ps, top, pgrep, and
conky gracefully handle EPERM/ENOENT and behave as if the current user is
the only user running processes.  pstree shows the process subtree which
contains "pstree" process.

Note: the patch doesn't deal with setui…
0499680a4214.subject: procfs: add hidepid= and gid= mount options

08389d888287.author: Daniel Borkmann
08389d888287.body:
Add a kconfig knob which allows for unprivileged bpf to be disabled by default.
If set, the knob sets /proc/sys/kernel/unprivileged_bpf_disabled to value of 2.

This still allows a transition of 2 -> {0,1} through an admin. Similarly,
this also still keeps 1 -> {1} behavior intact, so that once set to permanently
disabled, it cannot be undone aside from a reboot.

We've also added extra2 with max of 2 for the procfs handler, so that an admin
still has a chance to toggle between 0 <-> 2.

Either way, as an additional alternative, applications can make use of CAP_BPF
that we added a while ago.

Signed-off-by: Daniel Borkmann <daniel@iogearbox.net>
Signed-off-by: Alexei Starovoitov <ast@kernel.org>
Link: https://lore.kernel.org/bpf/74ec548079189e4e4dffaeb42b8987bb3c852eee.1620765074.git.daniel@iogearbox.net
08389d888287.subject: bpf: Add kconfig knob for disabling unpriv bpf by default

0887a7ebc977.author: Kees Cook
0887a7ebc977.body:
Patch series "ubsan: Split out bounds checker", v5.

This splits out the bounds checker so it can be individually used.  This
is enabled in Android and hopefully for syzbot.  Includes LKDTM tests for
behavioral corner-cases (beyond just the bounds checker), and adjusts
ubsan and kasan slightly for correct panic handling.

This patch (of 6):

The Undefined Behavior Sanitizer can operate in two modes: warning
reporting mode via lib/ubsan.c handler calls, or trap mode, which uses
__builtin_trap() as the handler.  Using lib/ubsan.c means the kernel image
is about 5% larger (due to all the debugging text and reporting structures
to capture details about the warning conditions).  Using the trap mode,
the image size changes are much smaller, though at the loss of the
"warning only" mode.

In order to give greater flexibility to system builders that want minimal
changes to image size and are prepared to deal with kernel code being
aborted and potentially destabilizing the system, this introduces
CONFIG_UBSAN_TRAP.  The resulting image sizes comparison:

   text    data     bss       dec       hex     filename
19533663   6183037  18554956  44271656  2a38828 vmlinux.stock
19991849   7618513  18874448  46484810  2c54d4a vmlinux.ubsan
19712181   6284181  18366540  44362902  2a4ec96 vmlinux.ubsan-trap

CONFIG_UBSAN=y:      image +4.8% (text +2.3%, data +18.9%)
CONFIG_UBSAN_TRAP=y: image +0.2% (text +0.9%, data +1.6%)

Additionally adjusts the CONFIG_UBSAN Kconfig help for clarity and removes
the mention of non-existing boot param "ubsan_handle".

0b24becc810d.author: Andrey Ryabinin
0b24becc810d.body:
Kernel Address sanitizer (KASan) is a dynamic memory error detector.  It
provides fast and comprehensive solution for finding use-after-free and
out-of-bounds bugs.

KASAN uses compile-time instrumentation for checking every memory access,
therefore GCC > v4.9.2 required.  v4.9.2 almost works, but has issues with
putting symbol aliases into the wrong section, which breaks kasan
instrumentation of globals.

This patch only adds infrastructure for kernel address sanitizer.  It's
not available for use yet.  The idea and some code was borrowed from [1].

Basic idea:

The main idea of KASAN is to use shadow memory to record whether each byte
of memory is safe to access or not, and use compiler's instrumentation to
check the shadow memory on each memory access.

Address sanitizer uses 1/8 of the memory addressable in kernel for shadow
memory and uses direct mapping with a scale and offset to translate a
memory address to its corresponding shadow address.

Here is function to translate address to corresponding shadow address:

     unsigned long kasan_mem_to_shadow(unsigned long addr)
     {
                return (addr >> KASAN_SHADOW_SCALE_SHIFT) + KASAN_SHADOW_OFFSET;
     }

where KASAN_SHADOW_SCALE_SHIFT = 3.

So for every 8 bytes there is one corresponding byte of shadow memory.
The following encoding used for each shadow byte: 0 means that all 8 bytes
of the corresponding memory region are valid for access; k (1 <= k <= 7)
means that the first k bytes are valid for access, and other (8 - k) bytes
are not; Any negative value indicates that the entire 8-bytes are
inaccessible.  Different negative values used to distinguish between
different kinds of inaccessible memory (redzones, freed memory) (see
mm/kasan/kasan.h).

To be able to detect accesses to bad memory we need a special compiler.
Such compiler inserts a specific function calls (__asan_load*(addr),
__asan_store*(addr)) before each memory access of size 1, 2, 4, 8 or 16.

These functions check whether memory re…
0b24becc810d.subject: kasan: add kernel address sanitizer infrastructure

0c92385dc05e.author: Peter Zijlstra
0c92385dc05e.body:
While WAIT_FOR_ENDBR is specified to be a full speculation stop; it
has been shown that some implementations are 'leaky' to such an extend
that speculation can escape even the FineIBT preamble.

To deal with this, add additional hardening to the FineIBT preamble.

Notably, using a new LLVM feature:

  https://github.com/llvm/llvm-project/commit/e223485c9b38a5579991b8cebb6a200153eee245

which encodes the number of arguments in the kCFI preamble's register.

Using this register<->arity mapping, have the FineIBT preamble CALL
into a stub clobbering the relevant argument registers in the
speculative case.

Scott sayeth thusly:

Microarchitectural attacks such as Branch History Injection (BHI) and
Intra-mode Branch Target Injection (IMBTI) [1] can cause an indirect
call to mispredict to an adversary-influenced target within the same
hardware domain (e.g., within the kernel). Instructions at the
mispredicted target may execute speculatively and potentially expose
kernel data (e.g., to a user-mode adversary) through a
microarchitectural covert channel such as CPU cache state.

CET-IBT [2] is a coarse-grained control-flow integrity (CFI) ISA
extension that enforces that each indirect call (or indirect jump)
must land on an ENDBR (end branch) instruction, even speculatively*.
FineIBT is a software technique that refines CET-IBT by associating
each function type with a 32-bit hash and enforcing (at the callee)
that the hash of the caller's function pointer type matches the hash
of the callee's function type. However, recent research [3] has
demonstrated that the conditional branch that enforces FineIBT's hash
check can be coerced to mispredict, potentially allowing an adversary
to speculatively bypass the hash check:

__cfi_foo:
  ENDBR64
  SUB R10d, 0x01234567
  JZ foo    # Even if the hash check fails and ZF=0, this branch could still mispredict as taken
  UD2
foo:
  ...

The techniques demonstrated in [3] require the attacker to be able to
control the contents of at least…
0c92385dc05e.subject: x86/ibt: Implement FineIBT-BHI mitigation

0ce20dd84089.author: Alexander Potapenko
0ce20dd84089.body:
Patch series "KFENCE: A low-overhead sampling-based memory safety error detector", v7.

This adds the Kernel Electric-Fence (KFENCE) infrastructure. KFENCE is a
low-overhead sampling-based memory safety error detector of heap
use-after-free, invalid-free, and out-of-bounds access errors.  This
series enables KFENCE for the x86 and arm64 architectures, and adds
KFENCE hooks to the SLAB and SLUB allocators.

KFENCE is designed to be enabled in production kernels, and has near
zero performance overhead. Compared to KASAN, KFENCE trades performance
for precision. The main motivation behind KFENCE's design, is that with
enough total uptime KFENCE will detect bugs in code paths not typically
exercised by non-production test workloads. One way to quickly achieve a
large enough total uptime is when the tool is deployed across a large
fleet of machines.

KFENCE objects each reside on a dedicated page, at either the left or
right page boundaries. The pages to the left and right of the object
page are "guard pages", whose attributes are changed to a protected
state, and cause page faults on any attempted access to them. Such page
faults are then intercepted by KFENCE, which handles the fault
gracefully by reporting a memory access error.

Guarded allocations are set up based on a sample interval (can be set
via kfence.sample_interval). After expiration of the sample interval,
the next allocation through the main allocator (SLAB or SLUB) returns a
guarded allocation from the KFENCE object pool. At this point, the timer
is reset, and the next allocation is set up after the expiration of the
interval.

To enable/disable a KFENCE allocation through the main allocator's
fast-path without overhead, KFENCE relies on static branches via the
static keys infrastructure. The static branch is toggled to redirect the
allocation to KFENCE.

The KFENCE memory pool is of fixed size, and if the pool is exhausted no
further KFENCE allocations occur. The default config is conservative
with only…
0ce20dd84089.subject: mm: add Kernel Electric-Fence infrastructure

0d5936344f30.author: Tejun Heo
0d5936344f30.body:
There are a couple interface issues which can be addressed in cgroup2
interface.

* Stats from cpuacct being reported separately from the cpu stats.

* Use of different time units.  Writable control knobs use
  microseconds, some stat fields use nanoseconds while other cpuacct
  stat fields use centiseconds.

* Control knobs which can't be used in the root cgroup still show up
  in the root.

* Control knob names and semantics aren't consistent with other
  controllers.

This patchset implements cpu controller's interface on cgroup2 which
adheres to the controller file conventions described in
Documentation/cgroups/cgroup-v2.txt.  Overall, the following changes
are made.

* cpuacct is implictly enabled and disabled by cpu and its information
  is reported through "cpu.stat" which now uses microseconds for all
  time durations.  All time duration fields now have "_usec" appended
  to them for clarity.

  Note that cpuacct.usage_percpu is currently not included in
  "cpu.stat".  If this information is actually called for, it will be
  added later.

* "cpu.shares" is replaced with "cpu.weight" and operates on the
  standard scale defined by CGROUP_WEIGHT_MIN/DFL/MAX (1, 100, 10000).
  The weight is scaled to scheduler weight so that 100 maps to 1024
  and the ratio relationship is preserved - if weight is W and its
  scaled value is S, W / 100 == S / 1024.  While the mapped range is a
  bit smaller than the orignal scheduler weight range, the dead zones
  on both sides are relatively small and covers wider range than the
  nice value mappings.  This file doesn't make sense in the root
  cgroup and isn't created on root.

* "cpu.weight.nice" is added. When read, it reads back the nice value
  which is closest to the current "cpu.weight".  When written, it sets
  "cpu.weight" to the weight value which matches the nice value.  This
  makes it easy to configure cgroups when they're competing against
  threads in threaded subtrees.

* "cpu.cfs_quota_us" and "cpu.cfs_period_…
0d5936344f30.subject: sched: Implement interface for cgroup unified hierarchy

0db2e5d18f76.author: Robin Murphy
0db2e5d18f76.body:
Taking inspiration from the existing arch/arm code, break out some
generic functions to interface the DMA-API to the IOMMU-API. This will
do the bulk of the heavy lifting for IOMMU-backed dma-mapping.

Since associating an IOVA allocator with an IOMMU domain is a fairly
common need, rather than introduce yet another private structure just to
do this for ourselves, extend the top-level struct iommu_domain with the
notion. A simple opaque cookie allows reuse by other IOMMU API users
with their various different incompatible allocator types.

106a4ee258d1.author: Rusty Russell
106a4ee258d1.body:
We do a very simple search for a particular string appended to the module
(which is cache-hot and about to be SHA'd anyway).  There's both a config
option and a boot parameter which control whether we accept or fail with
unsigned modules and modules that are signed with an unknown key.

If module signing is enabled, the kernel will be tainted if a module is
loaded that is unsigned or has a signature for which we don't have the
key.

(Useful feedback and tweaks by David Howells <dhowells@redhat.com>)

Signed-off-by: Rusty Russell <rusty@rustcorp.com.au>
Signed-off-by: David Howells <dhowells@redhat.com>
Signed-off-by: Rusty Russell <rusty@rustcorp.com.au>

10e9ae9fabaf.author: Alexander Popov
10e9ae9fabaf.body:
The STACKLEAK feature erases the kernel stack before returning from
syscalls. That reduces the information which kernel stack leak bugs can
reveal and blocks some uninitialized stack variable attacks.

This commit introduces the STACKLEAK gcc plugin. It is needed for
tracking the lowest border of the kernel stack, which is important
for the code erasing the used part of the kernel stack at the end
of syscalls (comes in a separate commit).

The STACKLEAK feature is ported from grsecurity/PaX. More information at:
  https://grsecurity.net/
  https://pax.grsecurity.net/

This code is modified from Brad Spengler/PaX Team's code in the last
public patch of grsecurity/PaX based on our understanding of the code.
Changes or omissions from the original code are ours and don't reflect
the original grsecurity/PaX code.

Signed-off-by: Alexander Popov <alex.popov@linux.com>
Tested-by: Laura Abbott <labbott@redhat.com>
Signed-off-by: Kees Cook <keescook@chromium.org>
10e9ae9fabaf.subject: gcc-plugins: Add STACKLEAK plugin for tracking the kernel stack

156ff4a544ae.author: Peter Zijlstra
156ff4a544ae.body:
Add Kconfig, Makefile and basic instruction support for x86 IBT.

(Ab)use __DISABLE_EXPORTS to disable IBT since it's already employed
to mark compressed and purgatory. Additionally mark realmode with it
as well to avoid inserting ENDBR instructions there. While ENDBR is
technically a NOP, inserting them was causing some grief due to code
growth. There's also a problem with using __noendbr in code compiled
without -fcf-protection=branch.
